entities.name: Munich Handbook (CLM 849)
entities.raw_quote: ...the Munich handbook in any event gives four experiments for obtaining a spirit in the form of a horse...

entities.name: experiment no. 9 (horse ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...the most extended of these (no. 9): I also wish to explain to you how to obtain a horse – that is, a spirit in the form of a horse...

entities.name: experiment no. 14 (horse ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...In one experiment (no. 14) the master looks toward the eastern sky, kneeling and with folded hands...

entities.name: experiment no. 17 (horse ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...Another experiment (no. 17) is exceptional in that it does not begin with a conjuration, but with merely the inscription of magic names.

entities.name: experiment no. 43 (horse ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...In the third of these simple experiments (no. 43) the master again writes a series of names on the door of a vacant house, at dusk, with bat’s blood...

entities.name: experiment no. 8 (ship ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...the Munich handbook provides two similar ones... for conjuring a magical ship (no. 8)...

entities.name: experiment no. 15 (flying throne ritual)
entities.raw_quote: ...the greater part of the elaborate and fanciful experiment to obtain a flying throne (no. 15) is a ritual for summoning the throne itself...

entities.name: experiment no. 11 (invisibility cloak)

attributes.timing: Wednesday, first hour of day, waxing moon

entities.raw_quote: I shall treat also of the art of invisibility... When you wish to become invisible and insensible to all beings...

entities.name: experiment no. 21 (invisibility)
entities.raw_quote: ...the first (no. 21) because the techniques are without parallel in this manual...

entities.name: experiment no. 45 (invisibility)
entities.raw_quote: ...the second (no. 45) because it not only entails a sacrificial offering to the demons but explicitly states that these spirits are worthy of sacrifice.

entities.name: experiment no. 10 (life/death manipulation)

attributes.purpose: make living appear dead or dead appear alive

entities.raw_quote: ...the Munich handbook comes closest in an experiment (no. 10) by which the necromancer can make a living person appear dead or a dead person appear alive...

relationships.from: magic circle with names and figures

summary: This chunk details several ritual experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), including methods for obtaining illusory transportation (horses, ships, flying throne), invisibility (via cloak, plant, or sacrifice), and manipulation of life/death states using inscribed rings. It provides detailed conjurations, spirit names (e.g., Lautrayth, Feremin, Oliroomim; Firiel, Melemil, Berith, Taraor; Brimer, Suburith, Tranauit, Lyroth, Beryen, Damayn), protective circles, ritual ingredients (bridle, bat’s blood, horsehide, heliotrope seeds), and biblical references used in incantations. The text also notes connections to other traditions like the Rawlinson manuscript and classical motifs such as the ring of Gyges.
key_passages: O Lautrayth, Feremin, [and] Oliroomim, spirits who attend upon sinners, I, so-and-so, trusting in your power, conjure you by Him who spoke and [all things] were made...
When you have said this three times, you will see three knights come from afar... they will at once dismount from their horses and greet you cheerfully.
I conjure you, O Fyriel, Mememil, Berith, [and] Taraor, powerful, magnificent, illustrious spirits, in whom I place all my trust, by the one, inseparable and undivided Trinity...

When you wish to infuse a spirit into a dead person... have a ring made of gold. Around the outside these names should be carved: Brimer, Suburith, Tranauit; on the inside, these names: Lyroth, Beryen, Damayn.
...if the circle given above is inscribed on horsehide with the blood of a horse and with the tooth of a white horse, and then is shown to horses, they will die at once.
